snippets.title: MSE and MAE from scratch
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

y_true = np.array([10.0, 12.0, 8.0])
y_pred = np.array([9.0, 15.0, 8.5])

mse = np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)
mae = np.mean(np.abs(y_true - y_pred))

print("MSE:", mse)
print("MAE:", mae)
snippets.explanation: Batch MSE averages squared residuals; MAE averages absolute residuals. Same predictions, different penalty on the large error at index 1.

snippets.title: Binary cross-entropy (sigmoid output)
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

y = 1.0          # true label
p_hat = 0.2      # predicted P(y=1)

bce = -(y * np.log(p_hat) + (1 - y) * np.log(1 - p_hat))
print("BCE:", bce)
snippets.explanation: BCE is H(P, Q) for Bernoulli P (label) and Q (sigmoid prediction). See Cross-entropy topic for the general H(P, Q) definition.

snippets.title: Categorical cross-entropy (softmax)
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

logits = np.array([2.0, 1.0, 0.5])
probs = np.exp(logits - logits.max())
probs /= probs.sum()

y_true = 1  # class index
ce = -np.log(probs[y_true])
print("softmax:", probs)
print("CE:", ce)
snippets.explanation: For one-hot P, CE reduces to −log q(y). Softmax converts logits to class probabilities Q.

snippets.title: sklearn and PyTorch loss APIs
snippets.code:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, log_loss
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Regression
y_true = [3.0, 5.0, 2.5]
y_pred = [2.8, 5.5, 2.4]
print("sklearn MSE:", mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred))
print("sklearn MAE:", mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred))

# Classification
logits = torch.tensor([[2.0, 1.0, 0.5]])
target = torch.tensor([1])
print("PyTorch CE:", nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(logits, target).item())
snippets.explanation: Frameworks expose the same four losses under familiar names. CrossEntropyLoss expects raw logits, not softmax probabilities.
